pattern: Decide x one bit at a time from the most significant of the n free bits down to the least: where a and b already share a bit, flipping it identically only ever helps, and where they differ, handing the 1-bit to whichever of the two running values is currently smaller keeps them balanced -- and a fixed-sum product is maximized when the factors are as close together as possible.
hints: x can only affect the lowest n bits of a and b -- every bit above that is already fixed and untouchable.
For a bit position where a and b already agree, flipping it (or not) changes both numbers identically -- do whichever raises both, since it can never create an imbalance.

For a bit position where a and b differ, the pair of resulting bits is always {0, 1} no matter what x picks -- so the real decision is only which of the two numbers gets the 1, and giving it to whichever running value is currently smaller keeps the two factors as close as possible, which is what maximizes their product for a fixed sum.

edge_inputs.description: a is zero, b is not -- tests the tie-break direction
n larger than the bit-length of both numbers
n large enough that x ranges over a million-plus candidates

stress.description: random small a, b, n with a full brute-force-friendly range for cross-checking at build time, and a wider spread of magnitudes
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(0, min(40, max(1, size)))
    a = rng.randint(0, 2 ** 40)
    b = rng.randint(0, 2 ** 40)
    return [a, b, n]
